text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Abhishek from AWS and with me today I have
Sarath from Intellect Design. Welcome Sarath. Thanks Abhishek, how are you? Great. So Sarath,
tell me about Intellect Design. Intellect Design is a FinTech and Insutech company. We provide
different softwares for banking organization, insurance carriers, and risk and treasury
organizations. So our job is to make the customer experience delightful. Fantastic.
And we're going to talk about one of your products today. That's correct, yeah. So what
does the product do? So Intellect Fabric Data Service is the product what we're talking. So
the idea is that the challenge what we have in the current insurance world is
that you've got multiple sources of data in different forms and places, okay. So the
Fabric Data Platform is a big data platform which actually is the world's
largest insurance carriers data sources and it can actually provide data on different entities
or corporations or persons of its heart. So we've got the Fabric Data Platform currently
already have built in 2,000 data sources and what we provide is for the insurance carriers
or the underwriters where they can search, they can match, aggregate, triangulate the data,
and then provide a particular entity score on a risk analysis basis. I see. So you're basically
going through all sorts of data which is out there, finding insights into it, places that
building profiles for individuals and then passing it on to your clients which are primarily
insurance providers who are able to take better business decisions basis those inputs.
Absolutely. Sounds fantastic. So take us through how you built the product.
So we've got three different types of data sources. I mean, one is that we actually go and crawl
different data sources like your social media or it could be wellness or it could be regulatory
and company related data. And the second thing where we do is we also connect to the premium
connectors. Those connectors provide a lot of crucial data where it's already processed data of its heart.
The third is basing on different carriers, the tier one, tier two, basing on their requirements,
we create data connectors and we actually customize that for them. So in the first case where we got
multiple data sources, all of that particular data actually is passing on to the ELB. And the ELB,
basing on the number of data sources we crawl or we connect to the premium, it fans out into multiple
managed services of the chart. And once the ELB actually does it, it actually pushes the entire thing
onto the Kubernetes cluster which has got multiple microservices within itself.
So these microservices are essentially what are consuming these events that are coming. So what sort of scale are we talking about here?
Yeah. So the Kubernetes cluster at any point of time actually when you crawl those multiple data sources has got one to two million messages at any point of time which it processes.
Quite a bit.

Yeah. It has got multiple microservices. It could be crawler surveys, JSON mapper, it could be Ddup surveys. All of that will be actually working within the Kubernetes cluster. And it actually pushes the data into the MQ because of different latency factors, what it takes.

And once the process data is there, I mean actually the MQ process data, once it's gone through the pipeline, data pipeline, where our machine learning algorithms uses Jupyter Notebooks, we cleanse the data, we triangulate, at the same time we do contextual analysis and sentiment analysis.
So the process data is the one which actually gets pushed onto your Mongo Atlas.
Interesting. So you basically have different microservices here and you've loosely coupled them using Amazon MQ. That's great actually.
And you're using machine learning as part of your setup as well to gain much more insights into it. That's great. Interesting choice of database I see here.
Yeah. So what led to that design?
So, I don't know, three years back when we were doing the product engineering, I think we were using MongoDB on EC2 instances.
I see.
The overhead of using MongoDB on EC2 is that you need to maintain the snapshots, backups, archive it, and you need to follow the regulatory guidelines.
At the same time, you have to keep up with the versioning, right? I think that's where a lot of our operations team have been busy with that.

So while we were engineering with the MongoDB team, I think we found out there's a beautiful solution with MongoDB, which is hosted on AWS, where we can do the VPC peering and connect it to your Kubernetes cluster.
Fantastic. So essentially this is running on AWS.
That's correct.
But because you're using VPC peering, so you have taken security into account here.
Yep.
The calls don't have to go over the internet.
They're still, everything remains within AWS itself.
That's correct.
Great.
You know, I see you're using RDS and Redis here as well.
Yep.
Tell us a bit about what they are used for.
Yeah.
That's the most important thing where our consumers are there.
We've got different underwriters.
We've got different agents, policy agents, or it could be insurance carriers.
So these are your clients basically.
Exactly.
Yes.
So what they do is they're the ones which connect to our Kubernetes cluster to the Kong, to the Kong API gateway.
Okay.
The Kong API gateway uses RDS, which is Postgres RDS.
Okay.
And all the authentication happens to the key clock, which is also a microservice which is sitting in Kubernetes cluster.
Okay.
And it also connects to the RDS.
So at any point of time, let's say you've got 100 underwriters calling in for multiple reports, you would probably need around 100 odd key clock services running and processing the data.
So the beauty about it is that Kubernetes provides the pod level auto scaling, while RDS provides the managed service from the Postgres.
Then Amazon provides the auto scaling on that one.
Fantastic.
So you're able to scale at the app level, you're able to scale at the database level as well.
Yeah.
And what role does Redis play here?
So Redis, what it does is it uses the, we use it for the cache management.
Okay.
All the user session management is done by Redis.
So within the Kubernetes cluster, we got close to 200 microservices.
Okay.
All of these microservices need to talk to different RDS.
It could be talking to API gateway of the chart.
So I think Redis is where, I mean, you've got scale at which we can actually, number of sessions can be managed using the RDS.
Fantastic.
Fantastic.
So I think this is great.
You know, it's a great product.
It's solving a critical issue out there and all.
So what's next?
Yeah.
So as I was telling you, the core philosophy for us is going to be either self-healing has to be good.
It has to be managed service or serverless.
I mean, that brings that, I mean, when we adopted KITS around two years back, it was still running KITS or EC2.
The managed worker node, managed nodes of the sort, you still have the overhead of managing them, right?
And you still have the worst thing to be done.
The next bet for us is to move that into EKS.
I think that will help us to remove the management overhead of running the KITS cluster.
That's one.
And the second important thing is we've got the Jupyter Notebooks running on microservices or EC2 instances.
I think that's something what we wanted to leverage AWS SageMaker so that you can actually, our data scientists can have access to different algorithms which are provided as part of the SageMaker.
At the same time, it can be cost effective for us.
The other important thing is MongoDB Atlas.
I think we can start looking at DocumentDB, how it would help us to amend the whole solution and make it more AWS VPC specific traffic.
Right.
So I think there's a fairly strong theme which is coming out of it.
You want to focus more and more on your product, your core features.
Yep.
And want to leave, you know, offload the overhead of managing the infra onto us.
Yep.
That's correct.
This is great.
You know, this is, you know, very useful.
A lot of learnings out of it.
You know, thank you very much for coming today and sharing your architecture with us.
Thanks, Hapshik, for having you.
Sure.
And thank you very much for watching This Is My Architecture.
